serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried cabbage in Singapore is approximately 150g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion served alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Cabbage — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Stir Fried Cabbage (F14080041). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried cabbage. GI estimated at 15 based on raw cabbage GI (~10) adjusted upward slightly for cooking and minor sauce additions. Cabbage is a non-starchy cruciferous vegetable with very low available carbohydrate (3.82g/100g). GL per 150g serving = (15 × 3.82 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.86, rounded to 0.9. Analogous estimate grounded in University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable category.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 299mg/100g — borderline elevated for a vegetable dish due to soy sauce and oyster sauce additions
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100g or request preparation with reduced soy sauce and low-sodium alternatives to keep sodium intake below 200mg per serving; the dish's potassium (~200mg/100g) and dietary fibre (2.33g/100g) partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low available carbohydrate (3.82g/100g) with 2.33g dietary fibre and estimated GI of 15, yielding negligible glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried cabbage is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low glycaemic load (GL ~0.9 per 150g serving), high fibre content slowing glucose absorption, and low sugar (2.17g/100g); it can be consumed freely as a vegetable side dish provided sauce additions are minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8g/100g), no trans fat, high dietary fibre (2.33g/100g), and sulforaphane from cruciferous cabbage supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried cabbage is a heart-friendly vegetable dish with low saturated fat, meaningful dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction, and cabbage-derived phytochemicals with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties; sodium should be monitored and sauce quantities minimised for optimal cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sulforaphane and vitamin C from cruciferous cabbage with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cabbage provides sulforaphane and vitamin C (~25mg/100g) with anti-inflammatory properties relevant to RA immune regulation; saturated fat is very low (0.8g/100g) and fibre (2.33g/100g) supports gut microbiome health, though sodium should be reduced by limiting soy and oyster sauce to minimise RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried cabbage is a common Singaporean and Chinese home-cooked vegetable side dish prepared by wok-frying shredded or roughly chopped cabbage with aromatics such as garlic and sometimes ginger, seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce until fragrant and lightly wilted. The dish retains a mild crunch with a savoury, slightly sweet flavour profile. It is a staple accompaniment to rice-based meals across Singapore, Malaysia, and Chinese communities throughout Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, it provides 76 kcal per 100g with low carbohydrate (3.82g), moderate fat (5.7g) from cooking oil, and meaningful dietary fibre (2.33g) from the cabbage. Sodium is 299mg/100g, elevated by sauce additions. Cabbage is a cruciferous vegetable providing sulforaphane, vitamin C (~25mg/100g), and antioxidants with anti-inflammatory properties. The dish has a very low glycaemic index (~15) and negligible glycaemic load, making it suitable for diabetes management. It is appropriate for cardiovascular health due to low saturated fat and high fibre, though sodium should be monitored for hypertension. The dish is vegetarian and dairy-free but contains soy and shellfish allergens from typical sauce ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried cabbage?
Is stir-fried cabbage good for diabetics?
Can I eat stir-fried cabbage with high blood pressure?
What is the GI of cabbage?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (F14080041) with confidence 0.95. The record is an exact match for the dish. Saturated fat (0.8g/100g), potassium (~200mg/100g), and vitamin C (~25mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cabbage cooked data as supplementary reference, consistent with HPB primary data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney non-starchy vegetable category.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Stir Fried Cabbage (F14080041) as of 2026. Saturated fat (0.8g/100g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked cabbage (NDB 11110) as a supplementary reference consistent with HPB primary data. Potassium (~200mg/100g) and vitamin C (~25mg/100g) are indicative estimates from USDA FoodData Central cooked cabbage used in health context narrative only and not entered as confirmed per_100g values. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable category; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried cabbage with sauce exists in the published literature. Dietary tags from input (vegetarian, plant_based, low_calorie, keto) preserved; is_vegan set to false because oyster sauce is a typical ingredient. Halal status noted as likely halal for standard preparations but may vary by sauce brand.
